back: Two compound propositions are logically equivalent if they have the same truth value for all possible combinations of truth values for their component variables. Denoted as p ≡ q.
p ∧ T ≡ p and p ∨ F ≡ p, where T is a tautology (true) and F is a contradiction (false).
Laws used to simplify the negation of a conjunction or disjunction: ¬(p ∧ q) ≡ ¬p ∨ ¬q and ¬(p ∨ q) ≡ ¬p ∧ ¬q.
p ∨ (p ∧ q) ≡ p and p ∧ (p ∨ q) ≡ p.

front: What is the Domination (or Null) Law?
back: p ∨ T ≡ T and p ∧ F ≡ F. A disjunction with a true proposition is always true; a conjunction with a false proposition is always false.
